key_insight: The paper shows that mean-field games can be made computationally tractable by replacing distribution-matching costs with kernel MMD penalties estimated by unbiased random Fourier U-statistics, while still proving almost-sure convergence guarantees.
engineering_relevance: This provides a scalable learning-based framework for large-population control problems such as Schrödinger bridges and coordinated EV charging, with linear-in-batch-cost estimators and provable convergence.
method: Model running and terminal distribution costs with RKHS MMD penalties.
Estimate both costs from empirical samples using unbiased random Fourier U-statistics with linear batch complexity.
Train a neural-network drift for the controlled diffusion by stochastic gradient descent and prove sample-level almost-sure convergence.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical experiments on Schrödinger bridge problems up to 100 dimensions and on an electric-vehicle charging coordination task with heterogeneous vehicles.
watch_item: Performance depends on coupled tuning of the penalty strength, random-feature count, sample size, and optimization tolerance, so miscalibration may hurt both accuracy and the stated convergence rate.
paper_title: Kernel-based potential mean-field games with unbiased random Fourier $U$-statistics
